Operating a self-hosted crawler at scale

Running a crawler that hits more than a thousand pages a day stops being a project and starts being an operations problem. This article documents the lessons from a year of running such a system: what to instrument, what to throttle, what to retry, and what to give up on.

Concurrency

The first knob anyone reaches for is concurrency. Raising in-flight requests from 1 to 16 typically yields a 10x throughput bump; from 16 to 64 maybe another 2x; beyond 64 you are throwing money away and starting to look like a botnet. Pick a profile per CPU core (we use 8x on a modern host) and clamp the maximum to something reasonable like 192.

Two-tier concurrency works well: a global semaphore caps total in-flight requests, and a per-host semaphore (typically 4–8) caps requests against a single origin. The per-host cap is what stops you from accidentally DOS-ing a small site when your queue is full of its URLs. Without it, every retry storm becomes someone else's problem.

Politeness

Respect robots.txt by default. The CLI flag to disable it should be loud and opt-in. The same applies to crawl delay — when a host advertises one, honor it. Send a real User-Agent string that identifies you and provides a contact URL. Site operators who can identify your bot and message you are far less likely to block you than ones who see only an unidentified header.

Back off when you see 429 or 503. Back off harder when you see a Cloudflare challenge page or a captcha. There is no business value in trying to brute-force past anti-bot systems; the right move is to add the domain to a "review" bucket and decide whether you actually need it.

Rendering

Most pages are HTML. Most modern pages are HTML that does nothing useful until JavaScript runs. You will need a headless browser for at least 30% of real-world targets. Run it as a separate service, not in-process, so you can recycle crashed instances and rate-limit memory consumption.

Auto-switching between HTTP and Chrome is worth implementing: try plain HTTP first, count the fraction of "thin" pages (under some character threshold), and re-fetch via Chrome if the fraction exceeds 60%. Track the auto-switch rate as a per-domain metric — sites that switch frequently are candidates for explicit always-Chrome configuration.

Deduplication

You will discover the same URL multiple times. Canonicalize aggressively: lowercase the host, strip default ports, normalize percent-encoding, drop fragments. Even with canonicalization you will find many URLs that point to the same content (session IDs in the path, tracking parameters in the query string).

For content-level deduplication, hash the extracted text after stripping boilerplate. Build a Bloom filter of seen hashes per crawl, and skip duplicates. For longer-running indexing, store the hash in your vector store payload and dedupe at query time so you do not return four copies of the same passage.

Observability

Every crawl emits a stream of events: discovered URL, fetch start, fetch complete, extract, embed, store. Log each with a correlation ID per job. Aggregate by domain and by job. The two most useful dashboards are: success/thin/fail rate per domain over time, and end-to-end latency from URL-discovered to vector-indexed.

Alert on changes, not absolutes. A 5% failure rate on a domain that has been at 5% for months is not an alert; a domain that jumped from 0.5% to 5% overnight is. Use rate-of-change anomaly detection if you have the time; otherwise weekly review meetings will catch most regressions.

Failure modes

The failure modes you will spend most of your time on, in rough frequency order: anti-bot challenges, soft 404s (HTTP 200 with an empty page), redirect loops, malformed HTML that breaks your parser, content-type lies (HTML served as text/plain or vice versa), and pages whose main content is loaded by a websocket after a delay.

For each, write a detector that flags the symptom and a remediation that either retries with different settings or quarantines the URL for manual review. Building these detectors is the bulk of crawler engineering once the happy path is solid.

Example crawler-ops handbook.
